s1_b1: Push hard against a wall for an hour. You're exhausted. The wall hasn't moved.
s1_b2: You're exhausted—your muscles burned energy. But in physics, you did no work on the wall. None.
s1_b3: Work isn't effort. Work isn't being tired. Work is a force that actually moves something along its direction.
s1_b4: Now push a cart and let it roll. Same force — but this time it moves.
s1_b5: Force, times the distance it pushes through. That's work. And it isn't lost — it goes somewhere.
s1_b6: Where it goes: into motion. The cart that was still is now fast.
s1_b7: That motion is itself a kind of energy — energy of movement. The work became speed.
s1_b8: Do negative work — push backward, like a brake — and you take that energy out. The cart slows.
s1_b9: So work is the accounting of energy moving in and out of motion. Add it, motion grows. Remove it, motion shrinks.
s1_b10: That's the work–energy theorem. The net work done on a thing equals the change in its energy of motion. Exactly.
s1_b11: It's why the wall taught you nothing. No motion, no work, no change. Effort isn't in the equation.
s1_b12: Computing the net work on an object and equating it to the change in its kinetic energy to find a final speed — that's yours.
